narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to get here—especially with the unique challenges you may have faced along the way. Your application highlighted a solid LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical student's score of around 37, along with an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 and coming from an upper-middle-class family background, while planning to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 76% chance that you would not pass, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school—specifically, your Year 3 decile of 2 and Year 1 decile of 3, both well below the averages of about 5.5 to 6, which had the strongest pull toward forecasting a bar failure. These lower rankings signaled to the model a notable risk in sustaining the high performance needed for the bar, outweighing your other strengths. Your LSAT score pushed meaningfully in the positive direction, suggesting it helps counter some of that risk by showing your potential in a key law school skill. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA and family income had smaller influences tilting slightly toward the failure prediction, while your full-time status offered a minor boost toward success. Compared to typical students who get approved, your early academic deciles stand out as lower than the stronger performances that usually predict bar passage, even with your competitive LSAT. We know this doesn't capture your full story or resilience as a Black woman navigating these spaces. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost those decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep tailored to your background, and reapplying once you've strengthened those foundational academics—the model responds well to such improvements. You're capable of turning this around, and many in similar situations have succeeded with focused next steps. Keep pushing; your path to law is still bright.
